content: A simplified small-signal equivalent circuit of a BJT-based amplifier is given below. The small-signal voltage gain $V_o / V_s$ (in $\mathrm{V} / \mathrm{V}$ ) is_________.
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V_0}{V_s}=\frac{-\beta I_b \times R_L}{I_b\left(R_s+r_\pi\right)} \\ & \frac{V_0}{V_s}=\frac{-\beta R_L}{R_s+r_\pi} \end{aligned} $$

content: The ideal BJT in the circuit given below is biased in the active region with a $\beta$ of 100 . If $I_B$ is $10 \mu \mathrm{~A}$, then $V_{C E}$ (in Volts, rounded off to two decimal places) is ____________ .
explanation:
$$ \text { Since, } I_C=\beta I_B=100 I_B ; I_E=101 I_B \text {, } $$

$$ \text { By KVL: } \quad \begin{aligned} I_B & =0.01 \mathrm{~mA} \\ 10 & =5 \times 101 I_B+V_{C E}+3 \times 101 I_B \\ V_{C E} & =10-808 \times 0.01 \\ V_{C E} & =1.92 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

to be _________

width of B, is given by
For the amplifier circuit of figure, the transistor has a $$\mathrm\beta$$ of 800. The mid-band voltage gain $$V_0/V_1$$ of the circuit will be

In the circuit shown, the identical transistors Q1 and Q2 are biased in the active region with $\beta=120$. The Zener diode is in the breakdown region with $V_Z=5 \mathrm{~V}$ and $I_Z=25 \mathrm{~mA}$. If $I_L=12 \mathrm{~mA}$ and $V_{E B 1}=V_{E B 2}=0.7 \mathrm{~V}$, then the values of $R_1$ and $R_2$ (in $\mathrm{k} \Omega$, rounded off to one decimal place) are ___________ , respectively.
explanation:
In the given circuit, the identical transistors $ Q_1 $ and $ Q_2 $ are operating in the active region, with a current gain $\beta = 120$. The Zener diode is in the breakdown region, which stabilizes the voltage at $ V_Z = 5 \, \text{V} $ and has a current $ I_Z = 25 \, \text{mA} $. Given that the load current $ I_L = 12 \, \text{mA} $ and the base-emitter voltages $ V_{EB1} = V_{EB2} = 0.7 \, \text{V} $, we need to determine the values of the resistors $ R_1 $ and $ R_2 $ in $ \text{k}\Omega $.

Calculations:

Calculate Base Current $I_{B2}$:

$ I_{C2} = I_L = 12 \, \text{mA} $

$ I_{B2} = \frac{I_{C2}}{\beta} = \frac{12 \, \text{mA}}{120} = 0.1 \, \text{mA} $

Calculate Current $I_1$:

Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the collector of $Q_1$:

$ I_1 = I_Z + I_{B2} = 25 \, \text{mA} + 0.1 \, \text{mA} = 25.1 \, \text{mA} $

Determine $R_1$:

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop involving $ R_1 $, Zener diode, and the base-emitter junction of $ Q_2 $:

$ 20 \, \text{V} = 0.7 \, \text{V} + 5 \, \text{V} + I_1 \cdot R_1 $

$ I_1 \cdot R_1 = 20 - 5.7 = 14.3 $

$ R_1 = \frac{14.3}{25.1} \approx 0.5697 \, \text{k}\Omega \approx 0.6 \, \text{k}\Omega $

Calculate Emitter Current $I_{E2}$:

Since $ I_{E2} = I_{C2} + I_{B2} $ and using the relationship with $\beta$:

$ I_{E2} = (1 + \beta) \cdot I_{B2} = 12 \, \text{mA} + 0.1 \, \text{mA} = 12.1 \, \text{mA} $

Determine $R_2$:

Applying KVL in the loop involving $ R_2 $:

$ 20 = I_2 \cdot R_2 + 0.7 + I_1 \cdot R_1 $

Substituting known values:

$ I_2 \cdot R_2 = 20 - 0.7 - (25.1 \cdot 0.5697) $

Solving for $ R_2 $:

$ R_2 = \frac{20 - 0.7 - 14.3}{12.1} \approx 0.4 \, \text{k}\Omega $

content: Which of the following statements is/are true for a BJT with respect to its DC current gain $\beta$?

options.content: Under high-level injection condition in forward active mode, $\beta$ will decrease with increase in the magnitude of collector current.

Under low-level injection condition in forward active mode, where the current at the emitter-base junction is dominated by recombination-generation process, $\beta$ will decrease with increase in the magnitude of collector current.
$\beta$ will be lower when the BJT is in saturation region compared to when it is in active region.
A higher value of $\beta$ will lead to a lower value of the collector-to-emitter breakdown voltage.

explanation:
(i) For a BJT the plot of $\beta$ and magnitude of collector current is

From the above plot we can conclude that.

Under low level injection $\beta$ increases with increase in the magnitude of collector current.

Under high level injection $\beta$ decreases with increase in the magnitude of collector current.

Hence, option (A) is correct and option (B) is not correct.

(ii) Since, $\beta_{\text {sat }}<\beta_{\text {active }}$

i.e., $\beta$ is lower when BJT is in saturation region compared to when BJT is in active region.

Hence, option (C) is correct.

(iii) Breakdown voltage of collector to emitter region is given by

$$ B V_{C E O}=B V_{C B O}\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\right)^{1 / n} $$

From the above relationship. As $\beta$ increases breakdown voltage of collector to emitter decreases.

Hence, option (D) is correct.

Hence, correct options are (A), (C) and (D).

content: The base of an $n p n$ BJT $T 1$ has a linear doping profile $N_B(x)$ as shown below. The base of another $n p n$ BJT $T 2$ has a uniform doping $N_B$ of $10^{17} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$. All other parameters are identical for both the devices. Assuming that the hole density profile is the same as that of doping, the common - emitter current gain of $T 2$ is
explanation:
Given : For transistor $T 2$ uniform doping $N_B(x)=10^{17} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$

$$ \text { Linear doping profile } N_B(x) \text { of transistor } T 1 \text { is shown below, } $$

Since, current gain $\beta$ is given as,

$$ \beta=\frac{L_E N_E D_B}{W N_B D_E} $$

Now, if other parameters are constant, only $W N_B$ is variable, then

$$ \beta \propto \frac{1}{W N_B} $$

Where, $W N_B$ is area under the curve of doping profile.

Let, $W N_B=G_B$ then

$G_{B_1}=$ Area under the curve for transistor $T 1$ and

$G_{B_2}=$ Area under the curve for transistor $T 2$

$G_{B_1}=$ Area under $N_B(x)$ for $T 1=\int_0^W N_B(x) d x=\frac{\left(10^{17}-10^{14}\right) W}{2}$

and $G_{B_2}=$ Area under $N_{B_2}=N_B W=10^{17} \times W$

So, the ratio of current gains of transistor $T 2$ and $T 1$.

$$ \frac{\beta_2}{\beta_1}=\frac{G_{B_1}}{G_{B_2}}=\frac{\frac{\left(10^{17}-10^{14}\right) W}{2}}{10^{17} \times W}=0.4995 $$

Therefore, $\boldsymbol{\beta}_{\mathbf{2}} \boldsymbol{\approx} \mathbf{0 . 5} \boldsymbol{\beta}_{\mathbf{1}}$, i.e. current gain of transistor $T 2$ is approximately 0.5 times that of transistor $T 1$.

IIT has declared it as MTA.

As the correct answer of the question is missing in the given options, so it is impossible to attempt the question as it is a multiple choice question having negative marking.

In the first answer key, IIT has given its answer to be option (B), i.e. current gain of transistor $T 2$ is approximately 2 times that of transistor $T 1$.

If the given answer by IIT would be option (D), i.e. approximately 0.7 times, then it can be considered that they have purposely used the word "approximately" in the statement, but as they have given the option

(B) that is approximately 2 times, it leads to the conclusion that they have taken the inverse ratio while framing the options.

So, in final answer key IIT considered this question as a MARKS TO ALL.

content: In the voltage regulator shown below, $V_I$ is the unregulated input at 15 V . Assume $V_{B E}=0.7 \mathrm{~V}$ and the base current is negligible for both the BJTs. If the regulated output $V_0$ is 9 V , the value of $R_2$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
explanation:
Given for the voltage regulator

Unregulated input voltage $V_I=15 \mathrm{~V}$

Base to emitter voltage $V_{B E}=0.7 \mathrm{~V}$

Regulated output voltage $V_0=9 \mathrm{~V}$ and base currents are negligible.

Method 1

Given voltage regulator circuit can be drawn as,

For finding the value of $R_2$, we have to find the node voltage $V_x$

Applying KVL in loop,

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_x-V_{B E}-V_z=0 \\ & V_x=V_{B E}+V_z=0.7+3.3 \\ & V_x=4 \mathrm{~V}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \end{aligned} $$

By voltage division rule,

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_x=\frac{V_0 R_2}{R_1+R_2} \\ & V_x=\frac{9 R_2}{1000+R_2} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

Comparing equation (i) and (ii),

$$ \begin{aligned} & 4=\frac{9 R_2}{1000+R_2} \\ & 4000+4 R_2=9 R_2 \\ & R_2=800 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

content: For the BJT in the amplifier shown below, $V_{B E}=0.7 \mathrm{~V}, \frac{k T}{q}=26 \mathrm{mV}$. Assume that the BJT output resistance ( $r_0$ ) is very high and the base current is negligible. The capacitors are also assumed to be short circuited at signal frequencies. The input $V_i$ is direct coupled. The low frequency voltage gain $\frac{V_0}{V_i}$ of the amplifier is
explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is as shown below, } $$

Also given, $V_{B E}=0.7 \mathrm{~V}$ and $\frac{k T}{q}=26 \mathrm{mV}$

$r_0$ is very high $\Rightarrow r_0=\infty$ and base current is negligible $\Rightarrow I_B=0$

D.C. Analysis :

For D.C., all capacitors will be open circuited and A.C. sources will be turned OFF. So, the circuit becomes as shown below

Applying KVL in the input loop,

$$ \begin{aligned} & 0-0.7-I_C \times 20 \mathrm{k} \Omega+10=0 \\ & I_C=\frac{10-0.7}{20 \mathrm{k} \Omega}=0.465 \mathrm{~mA} \end{aligned} $$

Small signal equivalent circuit,

Voltage gain is given as,

$$ \text { Where } \quad \begin{aligned} A_V & =\frac{V_{\text {out }}}{V_{\text {in }}}=-g_m R_L^{\prime} \\ R_L^{\prime} & =10 \mathrm{k} \Omega \| 10 \mathrm{k} \Omega=5 \mathrm{k} \Omega \\ A_V & =-\left|\frac{I_C}{V_T}\right| \times R_L^{\prime} \\ A_V & =-\left|\frac{0.465}{26}\right| 5 \mathrm{k} \Omega \\ A_V & =-89.42 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (C).
